questions.question: Explain the Modeling stage in the graphics pipeline and describe how geometric representations of objects are created.
keyPoints: Modeling is the first stage in the graphics pipeline
It involves creating geometric representations using vertices, edges, and polygons
Objects are defined through meshes, parametric curves, or implicit surfaces
Common representations include polygon meshes, NURBS, and subdivision surfaces
The output is a set of geometric primitives (points, lines, triangles) with attributes

questions.question: Describe the Geometric Processing stage and list the key operations performed on vertices and primitives.
keyPoints: Geometric Processing operates on vertices and primitives
Model transformations: rotation, translation, scaling, and their combinations
View transformation positions the scene relative to the camera
Projection transformation maps 3D to 2D (perspective or orthographic)
Clipping removes primitives outside the view frustum
Lighting calculations may also be performed at this stage

questions.question: What is rasterization? Explain its role in converting geometric primitives into fragments and mention scan conversion.
keyPoints: Rasterization converts geometric primitives into fragments
Scan conversion determines which screen pixels are covered by each primitive
Each fragment contains attributes: color, depth, texture coordinates, normals
Point, line, and triangle rasterization have different algorithms
Fragments are candidates for becoming pixels after fragment processing
Fragment generation involves interpolation of vertex attributes across primitives

questions.question: Discuss the Fragment/Display Processing stage and explain the operations that determine final pixel values.
keyPoints: Fragment/Display Processing is the final stage before writing to framebuffer
Texturing applies surface patterns and details to fragments
Blending combines colors from multiple fragments for transparency effects
Depth testing uses z-buffer to determine visibility
Scissor and stencil tests provide additional fragment culling
Final operations write the processed fragment color and depth to framebuffer
Anti-aliasing may be applied to smooth edges

questions.question: Compare and contrast Geometric Processing and Rasterization stages in the graphics pipeline, highlighting their differences.
keyPoints: Geometric Processing operates at vertex level; Rasterization operates at fragment/pixel level
Geometric Processing: transformations, clipping, projection are applied to vertices
Rasterization: scan conversion produces fragments from primitives
Geometric Processing reduces scene to visible primitives within view frustum
Rasterization determines per-pixel coverage and interpolates vertex attributes
Geometric Processing is programmable (vertex shader); Rasterization is fixed-function
Fragment processing follows rasterization to determine final pixel values
